name: adobo black bean stew
instructions:
FINELY SHRED THE SOFRITO BASE: use a food processor to finely shred a combination of white onions, turnips, green bell peppers, celery, and garlic. Use any or all of them — the food processor turns this into a unified shredded vegetable base. Knife-and-board chunks are too coarse here; you want the vegetables to dissolve into the stew, not sit as discrete pieces.

SAUTE THE BASE: in a heavy pot with olive oil, sauté the shredded soffrito until softened and slightly browned. This is the structural backbone of the stew.

ADD THE CHIPOTLE: stir in chipotle chiles in adobo sauce.

BUILD THE STEW: add black beans (at least 2 cans, or 1 can black + 1 can white — see notes on quantity), light stock, oregano, and thyme. Simmer until everything is heated through and the stew has thickened.

FINISH: a splash of white vinegar or white wine vinegar at the end for kick. Adjust salt and black pepper to taste. Stir in chopped cilantro.

SERVE WITH: a dollop of greek yogurt or sour cream on top. Optional fresh thinly-sliced turnip or radish for crunch.
notes:
FINELY SHREDDED SOFFRITO IS THE TECHNIQUE: the food processor is doing real work here. A knife dice is too coarse — the vegetables stay discrete and the stew reads as 'beans with chunks' rather than 'beans in a unified savory base.' The processor reduces the soffrito to a paste-adjacent texture that dissolves into the stew during the sauté. Any combination of onions, turnips, green bell peppers, celery, and garlic works; use what you have.

QUANTITY MATTERS — WORTH-THE-DRIVE THRESHOLD: this stew isn't worth making with one can of beans. You want at least 2 cans of black beans, or 1 can black + 1 can white beans. The volume of beans relative to the soffrito is what makes the stew feel substantial — under-bean it and the dish reads thin. White beans alongside black is a real variation, not a substitute.

HERBS ARE CREATIVE TERRITORY — BLACK BEANS LOVE HERBS: oregano and thyme are both robust herbs that black beans take well (see the herbacious black beans recipe for the broader principle: black beans favor fresh herbs in a way pinto beans don't). Be heavy-handed here. Other herbs welcome too — this isn't a slot with a rigid herb axis.

NO OTHER MEXICAN CHILIES OR CHILI POWDERS: the chipotle in adobo carries the chile work here. Adding ancho, pasilla, guajillo, california, or any chili powder muddies the dish — they're for different mexican recipes (chile colorado is the obvious one). Stick to chipotle + adobo as the chile component.

VINEGAR FOR KICK: white vinegar or white wine vinegar both work. A splash at the end brightens the dish — black beans + chipotle can read heavy without the acid pop.

CUMIN AND TOMATO ARE CLASHING (CORIANDER IS OK): cumin in particular pulls the dish toward american chili territory; tomatoes (fresh, canned, or paste) do the same job from a different angle. Once either is in, the adobo + celery framing stops working — the dish wants cheddar cheese instead of greek yogurt, the trinity reads off, and what should be a clean herb-and-bean stew turns into a generic american chili with chipotle. Coriander, on the other hand, doesn't drag the dish to that bad place — it sits in secondary as a real option. The recipe is carried by herbs + the trinity + light stock + chipotle in adobo; those four are enough on their own.

DAIRY FINISH: greek yogurt or sour cream on top. Both work; greek yogurt is leaner and slightly tangier, sour cream is richer. The dairy is the right cooling element against the chipotle heat — not lime, not cream.
